content: CORRECTED diagnosis of dead bench SO-101 (on /dev/ttyACM1; Reachy Mini holds /dev/ttyACM0). Symptom: all motors collide at factory id 1 (read id1 -> COMM_RX_CORRUPT, ids 2-6 -> TIMEOUT). EARLIER LOCK THEORY WAS WRONG: the STS3215 EEPROM Lock register (addr55=1) does NOT block the ID write on fw 3.10 — proven by writing id 1->2 while Lock=1 and it took. The low-level write works: official `arm101 set-motor-id 1 --apply` moved a single isolated motor 2->1 and it persisted across a fresh process. REAL ROOT CAUSE = the setup-motors/arm-setup WALK collapses chained motors: it auto-detects whatever id is on the bus and writes new_id to detected_id, walking gripper=6 down to shoulder_pan=1, with NO guard that only one motor is present and NO read-back verify. On an assembled (chained) arm where all motors share id 1, every step writes its id to ALL motors at once; they move in lockstep and the final step (shoulder_pan->1) leaves ALL motors at id 1. Tool reports success throughout. CODE GAPS to fix: (1) setup/set-motor-id must detect >1 responder on the bus and refuse with guidance; (2) must read the id back after write and confirm the unique id stuck. FIX to bring arm alive: physically isolate each motor (unplug daisy-chain) and assign + verify one at a time; the bus fundamentally requires isolation. -7=COMM_RX_CORRUPT (collision), -6=COMM_RX_TIMEOUT (absent).

VALIDATED on bench STS3215 (fw3.10) on /dev/ttyACM1: single-motor id assignment + gentle motion both work end-to-end. (1) Official `arm101 set-motor-id N --apply` correctly writes+persists id on a SINGLE isolated motor (Lock=1 does NOT block it). (2) Gentle move recipe (live-confirmed, operator saw it move ~26deg and back, smooth): STS3215 motion registers = addr41 Acceleration(1B), addr42 Goal_Position(2B), addr46 Goal/Running Speed(2B). Used acc=20, speed=500, +/-300 ticks; present_load stayed ~230-236 during FREE gentle motion (this is the no-contact baseline -> the --gentle contact threshold must sit ABOVE ~250 free-motion load). enable_torque(False)=limp at end. NOTE bus.py has write_goal_position + enable_torque but NO write_goal_speed/accel helper -> add them (or raw write2ByteTxRx addr46 / write1ByteTxRx addr41) when building arm flex --gentle. Procedure to bring up the assembled arm: each remaining motor ships at factory id 1, so it MUST be assigned ALONE (connect one, set id, verify, next) else it collides with the already-assigned id-1 motor. links: arm101-duplicate-id-diagnosis-2026-06-30

ROOT CAUSE + FIX of arm101 dead-arm: id/baud EEPROM writes never opened the STS3215 Lock register (addr55), so on fw3.10 the write took effect LIVE but did NOT commit to EEPROM and reverted to the factory value on the next power-cycle. That is why every assigned id silently fell back to id 1 after assembly/power-off, leaving all 6 motors colliding at id 1. PROVEN on hardware: unlock(addr55=0) -> write id -> relock(addr55=1) makes the id survive a real power-cycle (motor read back id 2 cold). FIX APPLIED in arm101/hardware/bus.py: FeetechBus._set_lock(motor,locked) helper added; write_id_baudrate now does _set_lock(motor,False) before the baud+id writes and _set_lock(new_id,True) after (relock addressed to the NEW id since the id write moves the device address); write_baudrate wraps its write the same way (relock at same id). Updated 2 tests in tests/test_bus.py that asserted the old no-lock sequence; full suite 385 passing. NOTE: lock does NOT block the live write (earlier wrong theory) - it blocks PERSISTENCE only. Still-open follow-ups: set-motor-id/setup-motors have no read-back verify and no multi-motor-on-bus guard (these would have surfaced the silent revert); fold into the doctor hardware-probe spec. supersedes earlier confusion in arm101-duplicate-id-diagnosis-2026-06-30.

Live gentle-flex touch-detection VALIDATED on assembled SO-101 follower gripper (id6). Incremental compliant close (25-tick steps, ACC=15 addr41, speed=300 addr46, slow): present_load ramped 140 (moving) -> 208 -> 320; threshold 250 triggered TOUCH at pos 2979/load 320, backed off 70 ticks and held, then enable_torque(False)=limp. Tuning insight for arm flex --gentle: gripper free-motion load is HIGHER than a big joint (~140-208 even moving slowly, due to gear friction) so the contact threshold must be PER-JOINT (gripper ~250-300; bigger joints lower). This confirms the spec parked-unknown: present_load contact threshold needs per-joint calibration + --threshold override. Whole-arm read also confirmed: all 6 ids 1-6 respond clean (shoulder_pan1 shoulder_lift2 elbow_flex3 wrist_flex4 wrist_roll5 gripper6) ~12V each after the eeprom-lock fix. links: arm101-eeprom-lock-persistence-fix-2026-06-30 arm101-gentle-move-validated-2026-06-30

gentle_move samples present_load BEFORE the servo physically moves, and tracks commanded ticks instead of measured position — proven on hardware 2026-07-12 during the first-ever `arm explore` hardware run (t12). EVIDENCE: a single gentle_move on wrist_roll (id 5, 400-tick travel, /dev/ttyACM1) RETURNED after 71ms claiming final_position=3548, but the joint was still at its start (3148) — it had not moved one tick. Real travel took ~900ms, entirely AFTER the call returned. Load during actual travel peaked at 272; every internal load read happened in the first 71ms while load was ~0-68. ROOT CAUSE (arm101/hardware/gentle.py stepping loop): `bus.write_goal_position(m, next)` then IMMEDIATELY `read_info(m)["present_load"]` with zero dwell (grep confirms NO sleep/wait/settle anywhere in arm101/hardware/), and `current = next_position` is the COMMANDED value so the loop exits when goal-writes are exhausted, not when the arm arrives; final_position=clamped_target is asserted, never measured. step=25 ticks => a 512-tick probe is ~20 writes in ~40ms, matching the observed ~60ms/probe. CONSEQUENCES: (1) contact detection is BLIND to contacts caused by the move itself — it only catches PRE-loaded joints (why the smoke run’s only contact was the gripper reading a stale 396, and likely why t7 rigid-stop passed: the arm was pre-pressed against the stop and the Torque_Limit cap of 500 was the real backstop, NOT the load-watch); (2) every `arm explore` reachable verdict is asserted from commanded ticks, never verified — the v0.16/v0.17 map is fiction (smoke run: load_magnitude exactly 0 on 9 of 10 probes); (3) the PR #31 per-joint DEFAULT_CONTACT_THRESHOLDS were tuned against these bogus near-zero reads — wrist_roll free-motion load peaks at 272 vs its 180 threshold, so a correctly-sampled load-watch would false-trigger on free motion. Thresholds MUST be re-tuned against real travel load profiles after the fix. WHY 604 TESTS MISSED IT: tests/test_gentle.py fake bus sets self._positions[motor] = position and bumps load inside write_goal_position — the servo teleports and load materializes on the commanding call, so the suite is structurally blind to early-sampling. FIX SHAPE: poll present_position + present_load DURING travel until measured arrival or timeout; contact = load>threshold while position is not advancing; terminate on measured arrival, not on commanded ticks exhausted; then re-tune thresholds. BLOCKS: all `arm explore` mapping runs are worthless until this lands.

HARDWARE CONSTRAINT (found during t3 baseline run, 2026-07-12): STS3215 `present_load` SATURATES at the servo`s `Torque_Limit` (register 48). Proven twice on the follower gripper: with Torque_Limit=300 the load climbed and pinned at exactly 300; with Torque_Limit=600 it pinned at exactly 600. CONSEQUENCE FOR CONTACT DETECTION: `gentle_move` caps Torque_Limit to `_CONTACT_TORQUE_LIMIT = 500` for the duration of a move, therefore ANY per-joint contact threshold >= 500 can NEVER fire, no matter how correctly the load is sampled. Combined with the free-motion peak loads measured DURING travel (wrist_roll peaks at ~272 just accelerating through free space), the usable band for each joint`s threshold is strictly (free-motion peak, 500). Plan task t7 (threshold re-tuning) MUST derive DEFAULT_CONTACT_THRESHOLDS inside that band and verify it is non-empty per joint — that is exactly the risk parked as frame q1 / plan r1 (a joint whose free-motion transient overlaps its blocked load has NO usable threshold, and the stall rule alone must carry the contact decision). SECOND GOTCHA from the same run: Torque_Limit=300 is BELOW the gripper`s own gear friction (~320), so the gripper could not move at all — an under-torqued joint stalls in FREE SPACE and looks exactly like a contact. Any profiling run must keep Torque_Limit above each joint`s free-motion requirement or it measures friction, not contact.

MEASURED per-joint profile of the SO-101 follower (/dev/ttyACM1), 2026-07-12, 500-tick legs at speed 150 / accel 20 / Torque_Limit capped to the production 500. This is the t7 measurement half of the gentle_move fix.

FREE-MOTION PEAK LOAD (taken ONLY from legs that arrived cleanly — the floor of each joint`s contact-threshold band): shoulder_pan 88, shoulder_lift 92, elbow_flex 148, wrist_flex 96, wrist_roll 300, gripper 76. Ceiling is 500 (present_load saturates at Torque_Limit, which gentle_move caps to 500). So EVERY joint has a usable band (free peak, 500) — risk r1 / frame q1 does NOT fire, and the 500 cap stands (boundary c6 holds).

The PR #31 thresholds are mis-calibrated against this: wrist_roll`s threshold of 180 sits BELOW its 300 free-motion peak, so it would false-trigger contact on every move. gripper`s 380 is safe but very conservative (free peak only 76 — the ~320 figure in the docs is the CONTACT ramp, not free motion).

MOTION-ONSET LATENCY ~95-127ms: the servo does not begin moving for ~100ms after the goal write. A stall detector that fires inside that window reports a phantom contact on EVERY move (this really happened to a profiling script). The stall check must arm only AFTER the joint has begun to move (position changed >= ~6 ticks), never on a fixed timer. Retreating FROM a contact, onset stretches to ~1.0-1.2s.

TRAVEL IS SLOW AND UNEVEN: 500 ticks takes ~930ms on wrist_roll but ~3300ms on the shoulder joints. Probe timeout must be >= 5-6s. This sharpens risk r2: at ~3s/probe, arm explore`s default 2000-move budget is >1.5 HOURS, not the ~80s it appeared to take while the sampling bug meant the arm never actually travelled.

CONTACT IS A GRADUAL STALL, not an instant one: the joint keeps creeping (e.g. 3022 -> 3001) while load is already past threshold, before fully stalling. Working stall constants from the profiling run: eps = 2 ticks, N = 8 consecutive samples @ 25ms poll (~200ms), arrival tolerance 12 ticks.

REAL BOUNDARIES DISCOVERED (saturated load + refusal to advance): wrist_flex hard stop at 2223 going up (survives a raised 900 cap, so genuinely physical); shoulder_pan stall at 3401 going down; shoulder_lift stall at 3311 going down; gripper closed stop at 2945 (with a soft object in the mouth it stopped early at 3001). NOTE these are single-pose measurements — free-motion load is pose- and travel-distance-dependent (gravity on shoulder_lift), so peaks may differ at other poses; a fuller multi-pose sweep is unmeasured.

SAFETY GAP (found 2026-07-12 during a hardware `arm explore` run): when a gated motion verb dies on an unhandled exception, it leaves ALL SIX MOTORS ENERGIZED. Observed: explore died on `unexpected: SerialException: device reports readiness to read but returned no data (device disconnected or multiple access on port?)` and every joint was left with torque=1, shoulder_lift holding near the encoder limit (4070/4095), until torque was released BY HAND.

WHY IT HAPPENS: gentle_move holds torque DELIBERATELY — stop-and-hold is its contract and it must not go limp after a contact. That is correct. But it means any abnormal exit from a motion verb walks away from a powered arm with no supervision and no cleanup; a joint left pressing on something keeps pressing. gentle_move already caps+restores Torque_Limit in a finally, so the gap is at the VERB/CLI level, above the primitive.

TRIGGER in this instance was operator error (a second process opened the same serial port mid-run — never run `arm read` while `arm explore` holds the bus). But the RESPONSE to any fatal path should not be `leave six motors powered`: a bus timeout, a cable knock, or a KeyboardInterrupt has the same shape.

FIX SHAPE: gated motion verbs (arm flex, arm flex --demo, arm explore, arm setup) should safe the arm on ABNORMAL termination — release torque on every motor they energized and restore any capped Torque_Limit — while leaving a SUCCESSFUL gentle_move`s deliberate stop-and-hold untouched. Ctrl-C should release too. NOT YET FILED as a GitHub issue (needs the user`s go-ahead).

RECOVERY recipe if it happens: open the bus and call enable_torque(m, False) + write_torque_limit(m, 1000) for m in 1..6.

RISK r2 CONFIRMED (2026-07-12): `arm explore`s default budget/resolution are unusable now that gentle_move actually waits for the arm. Measured on the follower: a single probe costs SECONDS, not the ~40ms the pre-fix code appeared to take (it never really moved). A blocked probe costs contact-detect (~2s) + retreat settle (up to the 6s timeout floor, because retreating FROM a contact has ~1.0-1.2s onset latency), and every blocked cell then triggers a bounded multi-joint ESCAPE search which multiplies that. `_travel_timeout` scales with distance (2.0 + 2.0*ticks/120), so a long probe can bound at ~35s. A 150-move run mapped only 2 cells in ~25 minutes. The old default of 2000 moves would be MANY hours.

SECOND, DEEPER PROBLEM — the grid does not fit the arm`s actual free space. At resolution 512 there are 8 buckets per joint across 0-4095, but the follower`s measured free ranges are NARROW: shoulder_pan [~3430, 4095] and shoulder_lift [~3311, 4095] are each barely ONE bucket wide, and wrist_flex is capped at ~2223. So nearly every grid cell lies OUTSIDE the reachable space, almost every probe is a wall, and the flood-fill spends its whole budget ramming boundaries. (The arm is folded/resting so the shoulders cannot descend — the table is the wall.)

IMPLICATIONS for the follow-up issue: (a) re-size --max-moves/--resolution from real per-probe timings; (b) resolution should be derived from each joint`s REACHABLE range, not from the full 0-4095 encoder span — a fixed global grid wastes the entire budget when the free space is a small corner; (c) consider seeding the flood-fill from a CENTRAL pose rather than wherever the arm happens to be resting (starting at a corner means the first probes are all walls); (d) cache/short-circuit: once a joint is known blocked beyond X in a direction, do not re-probe cells past X.

NOT A BUG in the fix — this is the honest cost of a primitive that actually waits for the arm. The pre-fix explore was fast only because it never moved.

HARDWARE FINDING (2026-07-12, follower /dev/ttyACM1): elbow_flex`s 12-bit encoder WRAPS WITHIN ITS PHYSICAL TRAVEL. Probing it toward 4095 rotated it past the 4095->0 boundary; it read back as ~1 and settled at ~111. So its encoder value is NOT monotonic with its joint angle: position 111 is physically ABOVE 4095, not below its measured wall at 2020.

TWO CONSEQUENCES:
1. A [min,max] reachable range CANNOT represent it. Deriving one by sorting the two probe endpoints yields [21,2000] — which is exactly the UNREACHABLE region, i.e. the inverse of the truth. Any code that assumes lo<hi spans the reachable set is wrong for this joint.
2. gentle_move CANNOT CONVERGE on a goal across the wrap. Its arrival check is |present_position - goal| <= tolerance in LINEAR ticks, so when the reading flips 4095->0 the error jumps to ~4094 and never falls inside tolerance; the move burns its full timeout and exits having oscillated near the wrap. clamp_goal/_step_direction are likewise linear and have no wrap concept.

WORKAROUND used: record only the CONTIGUOUS, WRAP-FREE region (above the 2020 wall, stopping short of the wrap) — arm-explore-follower.map.json carries an explicit _caveat_elbow_flex.

REAL FIX is an encoder RE-ZERO (calibration) so the joint`s travel does not straddle the 0/4095 discontinuity — not a map entry and not a gentle_move patch. Until then, do NOT command elbow_flex across the wrap, and do not trust a linear range for it. NOTE this may affect other joints if they are ever re-zeroed badly; wrist_roll spans the full encoder range but rotates freely, so it is unaffected in practice.

DESIGN FINDING (2026-07-12, /think spec for issues #33/#34/#35): RE-ZERO CANNOT FIX wrist_roll, EVEN IN PRINCIPLE. Issue #35 recommended re-zeroing BOTH wrapping joints (elbow_flex, wrist_roll) so the 4095->0 encoder seam falls outside physical travel. That works for elbow_flex — it has real walls (measured span 2020), so the seam can be parked in its UNREACHABLE arc. It is IMPOSSIBLE for wrist_roll: wrist_roll was mapped as free through its whole range (measured [21,4073], no contact anywhere — it stopped only where the 0-4095 clamp stopped it, not at a wall), i.e. its travel covers the WHOLE CIRCLE. No choice of zero can move a seam out of a travel that includes every angle — re-zero only relocates the seam, it cannot evict it. USER DECISION: elbow_flex is RE-ZEROED; wrist_roll is SOFT-LIMITED instead (a software bound in arm_spec/the map, NOT an EEPROM angle-limit write) to under a full turn, creating a DEAD ARC that must CONTAIN the seam. Acceptance test = sweep wrist_roll end-to-end of its soft-limited range and assert present_position is MONOTONIC (no 4095->0 jump inside permitted travel). GENERAL RULE: for any joint, ask first whether its travel is bounded or continuous — a re-zero is only available to a BOUNDED joint. SECOND FINDING from the same session: bus.py implements NO offset/homing register today (grep: only ADDR 5 id, 6 baud, 40 torque, 41 accel, 42 goal_pos, 46 speed, 48 torque_limit, 55 lock, 56 present_pos) and the STS3215 offset mechanism is UNVERIFIED — so the re-zero wave is gated on a READ-ONLY spike (identify register address/width/EEPROM-vs-RAM/range, prove it survives a power cycle) before any EEPROM write. THIRD: a re-zero INVALIDATES every tick ever recorded for that joint (maps, thresholds, run-logs, arm_spec bounds), so reachability maps must carry a CALIBRATION IDENTITY and explore must REFUSE a map measured under a different calibration rather than silently misreading it.

BUILD FINDING (2026-07-12, #33 safety wave, PR #38 v0.19.0): THE OBVIOUS TORQUE-RELEASE CALL IS THE WRONG ONE. To de-energize a motor on an abnormal exit, route through bus.clear_overload(motor), NOT bus.enable_torque(motor, False). Both are the IDENTICAL wire write (Torque_Enable=0, addr 40) and differ only in how they read the RESPONSE: a servo latched in overload tags EVERY packet response with the overload bit (0x20) — including the response to the very write that clears the latch — so enable_torque() raises OverloadError on exactly the motor that most needs de-energizing. clear_overload() masks that bit and treats it as success (bus.py:1136). A crashed motion verb is a prime way to leave a motor latched, so this is the common case, not the exotic one.

SECOND FINDING (same wave): FakeBus.clear_overload used to record its write SEPARATELY instead of routing through FakeBus.enable_torque. Since the two are the same byte on the wire, a test subclass could intercept enable_torque and SILENTLY MISS the identical byte written by clear_overload — i.e. the fake modelled a bus that cannot exist, and would let a test PROVE a torque-disable never happened when on real hardware it did. Fixed: FakeBus.clear_overload now calls self.enable_torque and tolerates OverloadError. LESSON: when two bus methods are the same wire op, the fake MUST funnel them through one overridable method or its subclass seams lie.

THIRD: torque_guard semantics = HOLD ON SUCCESS, RELEASE ON ABNORMAL (user decision). Clean exit issues ZERO release writes so gentle_move stop-and-hold survives (a closed gripper must not drop its object); abnormal exit (any BaseException, so SIGINT included) releases. TorqueGuard.__exit__ is typed -> None (not -> bool) — Sonar S3516 BLOCKER on a constant `return False`; None is falsy so behavior is identical and the signature states the never-suppress guarantee.

FOURTH: TorqueGuard.disown() exists for arm setup ONLY — writing a new servo id to EEPROM addr 5 MOVES the servo bus address, so a guard still owning the old id would aim its release at a servo that no longer exists and falsely report a motor as possibly-energized.

HARD LIMIT (2026-07-12, found while drafting t4 hardware acceptance for #33): A PHYSICAL USB YANK CANNOT BE RECOVERED IN SOFTWARE, and I wrongly wrote it into the spec as an achievable acceptance criterion (honesty conditions h1/h25) before catching it. The SO-101 servos are powered from the 12V SUPPLY; USB carries only DATA. Unplug the cable and /dev/ttyACM* disappears — the host has NO channel to the arm, every torque-release write fails, and the motors keep holding their last commanded goal. No torque_guard, no finally, no context manager can release torque over a bus that no longer exists.

WHAT THE GUARD *CAN* COVER (all real, all fixed by PR #38): every abnormal exit where the PROCESS STILL HAS A BUS — SIGINT/Ctrl-C, any unhandled Python exception, and the ACTUAL #33 incident (a second process opening the port; the error was `device reports readiness to read but returned no data (multiple access on port?)` — the port is CONTENDED but still ours, so the release lands).

The only thing that could close the yanked-cable hole is a SERVO-SIDE watchdog (the STS3215 dropping torque by itself on comms loss). Whether one exists is UNKNOWN and is filed as issue #37 — it pairs with the offset-register spike (plan task t5) since both interrogate the same memory table. CAVEAT for that investigation: a watchdog that drops torque during a LEGITIMATE hold would break gentle_move`s stop-and-hold gripper contract, so any timeout must exceed any real hold or it is unusable.

GENERAL LESSON: before writing a testable acceptance criterion, check it is PHYSICALLY achievable. I asserted this one and the user confirmed it on my word; it would have cost a hardware session to discover.

STS3215 ENCODER-OFFSET REGISTER — verified facts (t5 spike, 2026-07-12, triple-sourced: Feetech SMS_STS.h/.cpp, Feetech FTServo_Python, LeRobot tables.py/feetech.py/encoding_utils.py). Register `Ofs` (LeRobot: Homing_Offset): ADDRESS 31 (OFS_L; 32 = OFS_H), 2 BYTES, encoding SIGN-MAGNITUDE WITH SIGN ON BIT 11 — NOT twos complement (wire = (sign<<11)|magnitude; -1073 -> 3121, +1073 -> 1073). RANGE +/-2047 (max magnitude (1<<11)-1). EEPROM (persistent) — sits under //---EEPROM--- in SMS_STS.h; SRAM starts at addr 40. Therefore needs the addr-55 Lock unlock->write->relock dance (same region as ID/Baud — the exact registers PR #21 proved silently revert without it). EFFECT: Present_Position = Actual_Position - Homing_Offset.

RANGE MATH for elbow_flex: its unreachable arc spans raw (126, 2020); the offset must land INSIDE that arc; midpoint H = 1073, |1073| <= 2047 with 974 ticks headroom. KEY INSIGHT: the required offset is bounded by WHERE THE UNREACHABLE ARC SITS, not by the travel length — which is why +/-2047 clears a ~2020-tick travel comfortably. LeRobot half-turn rule independently gives 1074.

SECOND MECHANISM: writing 128 to Torque_Enable (addr 40) triggers Feetech firmware midpoint calibration (CalibrationOfs). Needs no wrap-aware move — human hand-parks the joint, torque off, one byte — sidestepping the chicken-and-egg that you cannot COMMAND a move across a seam you have not fixed yet.

TRAP FOR ANY IMPLEMENTER: do NOT copy LeRobot write_calibration wholesale — it ALSO writes Min/Max_Position_Limit (addrs 9/11), which in servo mode CLAMP goals and would narrow the very reachable set you are recovering. Write ONLY addr 31.

UNRESOLVED CAVEAT (the whole ballgame): it is UNDOCUMENTED whether the firmware computes Present = (raw - Ofs) MOD 4096 (seam RELOCATES -> re-zero works) or plain signed subtraction (seam STAYS PINNED at the physical angle where the magnet rolls over -> re-zero achieves NOTHING). Present_Position is itself sign-magnitude on bit 15 in both Feetech and LeRobot, so negatives ARE representable. Evidence favours modular (LeRobot SO-101 calibration = literally this fix; community says centring PREVENTS overflow) but is circumstantial. SETTLED ONLY BY A HAND-SWEEP with torque off, checking monotonicity — reading the offset back proves it was APPLIED, NOT that the seam MOVED.

TWO TRAPS THAT WOULD PRODUCE A FALSE PASS when verifying an encoder re-zero (found building t8, PR #40, 2026-07-12). Both matter because the verification is the ONLY thing standing between us and shipping a re-zero that silently did nothing.

TRAP 1 — THE DISCONTINUITY THRESHOLD MUST BE ~500, NOT THE OBVIOUS 2048. The intuition is `a seam jump is ~4096 ticks, so anything over half that (2048) is a wrap`. WRONG: if the firmware does a plain signed subtraction AND read_position`s `& 0x0FFF` mask folds the resulting negative back into range, the observed seam jump is only ~1949 ticks. A 2048 threshold sails straight past it and reports PASS on a broken fix. Use 500 (sensor noise is a few ticks; there is a huge gap between noise and any real discontinuity).

TRAP 2 — A SHORT CLEAN SWEEP IS `INCONCLUSIVE`, NEVER A PASS. A hand-sweep covering 200 of 2202 ticks that saw no discontinuity has proven NOTHING — the seam simply was not in the swept region. Claiming a pass from it closes an open question with a lie. Require >=80% travel coverage before `no discontinuity found` is allowed to mean anything.

GENERAL LESSON: when a verification exists to settle an unproven premise, enumerate how it could report SUCCESS while the premise is false. Both traps here are of that shape.

RELATED DESIGN CALL THAT PAID OFF: rather than assume the favourable semantics, FakeBus models BOTH via `offset_wraps` (True = seam relocates, the premise; False = seam stays pinned, the pessimistic reading). The verb is tested against each, so if hardware settles it the wrong way the pessimistic behaviour is already built and tested, not discovered in production.

STATE OF THE arm explore REBUILD (2026-07-12, main @ v0.21.0, 1202 tests). Three PRs merged today off the converged spec docs/specs/2026-07-12-arm-explore-now-maps-the-arm-s-real-joint-space-it.md and its 21-task plan:

- PR #38 (v0.19.x) — CLOSES #33. torque_guard: motion verbs release the arm on any ABNORMAL exit (exception/bus fault/SIGINT). HOLD ON SUCCESS, RELEASE ON ABNORMAL (clean exit = ZERO release writes, so gentle_move stop-and-hold survives).
- PR #39 (v0.20.x) — `arm profile <joint>`: ramps Goal_Speed and finds the highest speed at which CONTACT DETECTION STILL WORKS (not merely the highest the servo survives). Structurally refuses to certify a speed on free motion alone.
- PR #40 (v0.21.0) — encoder linearity (#35 code, NOT yet hardware-verified): `arm rezero` + --verify, offset primitives (addr 31), wrist_roll soft limit + enforcement, and the t5 spike doc.

STILL OPEN AND WHY:
- #35 stays OPEN deliberately — the code shipped but the SEAM-EVICTION PROOF has not run on hardware. docs/hardware-rezero-procedure.md is the procedure; step 6 (torque-off hand-sweep of elbow_flex through its full travel, asserting monotonic position) is what settles whether the servo actually MOVES the seam. If it reports `seam-not-evicted`, the re-zero achieves nothing and wave 2a returns to the user for a re-decision (the ARCS fallback was explicitly NOT chosen).
- #34 (grid) — t16 (per-joint bucket sizes from span + target bucket COUNT) is BUILT on branch feat/explore-grid, NOT yet PR`d. t17 (bounds from a supplied map, EEPROM only as fallback) and t18 (honest probe-cost budget + pre-flight wall-clock estimate) are NOT built and are BLOCKED on t15 — the hardware run of `arm profile` that yields real per-joint speeds.
- #37 — does the STS3215 have a comms-loss torque watchdog? Filed because a physical USB yank CANNOT be recovered in software (12V powers the servos; USB is only data).

HUMAN-GATED HARDWARE STILL OWED: t4 (SIGINT + port-contention safety acceptance), t6 (re-confirm elbow_flex wrap BEFORE re-zeroing destroys the evidence), t5-hardware/t11 (the rezero power-cycle + seam sweep), t12/t15 (baseline timing + the arm profile run).

SETTLED ON HARDWARE (2026-07-12, follower /dev/ttyACM1, human present): THE ENCODER RE-ZERO WORKS, and the spike`s open caveat is CLOSED BY MEASUREMENT.

THE BIG RESULT — the STS3215 DOES reduce the corrected position MODULO 4096, so the offset genuinely RELOCATES the seam (it does not merely relabel positions). Proven by a torque-off hand sweep of elbow_flex, before/after, same joint same motion:
  Ofs=0    -> monotonic FALSE, 1 discontinuity, span 4093 (the wrap, captured)
  Ofs=1073 -> monotonic TRUE,  0 discontinuities, span 2196, largest jump 28 ticks
Issue #35 is FIXED for elbow_flex on the physical arm. The pessimistic reading (plain signed subtraction, seam stays pinned) is REFUTED.

FACTORY OFFSET IS 85, NOT 0. All six servos ship with Ofs=+85 at addr 31 (uniform across six joints => a vendor default, not a calibration). The t5 spike ASSUMED factory 0. CONSEQUENCE: every tick ever recorded on this arm was measured in the 85-shifted frame. This is WHY elbow_flex wraps — the reported seam sits where Actual==Ofs, i.e. at raw 85, which is BELOW the unreachable arc`s lower edge (raw 211) and therefore INSIDE the joint`s travel.

SEMANTICS CONFIRMED TWICE, reversibly: writing Ofs 85->185 dropped reported position by EXACTLY 100; zeroing 85->0 raised it by EXACTLY 85. So Present_Position = Actual - Ofs.

ELBOW_FLEX`S FAR WALL, MEASURED FOR THE FIRST TIME (nothing could see across the seam before): travel spans 2196 ticks, reading 1034..3230 in the corrected frame at Ofs=1073. Converting to RAW (raw = corrected + 1073 mod 4096): raw travel = [2107,4095] U [0,207], so the TRUE RAW UNREACHABLE ARC = (207, 2107), width 1900, midpoint 1157. The shipped table said (126,2020)/midpoint 1073 — those were REPORTED-frame ticks in the 85 frame. 1073 lands inside (207,2107) with 866/1034 margin, so the shipped code WORKED BY LUCK, not by construction.

ALSO VALIDATED LIVE: PR #38`s torque_guard fired on real hardware — a failing `arm rezero` released motor 3 on its way out (`Torque released on motors 3 after an abnormal exit`). And t8`s false-pass guards rejected THREE sweeps as INCONCLUSIVE (0, 0 and 376 ticks of coverage) before the real one — a naive `no discontinuity seen => PASS` would have declared victory on an empty sweep with the arm untouched.

STILL UNPROVEN: PERSISTENCE. The offset reads back as 1073 but has NOT survived a power cycle yet — PR #21 exists because exactly this read back fine and reverted on power-up. Cut and restore the 12V BUS POWER (not USB) and re-read addr 31.

GOTCHA: a read issued immediately after an EEPROM write can return GARBAGE — a read_position ~0.2s after write_offset returned 0 while the servo genuinely held 3387 (re-reads were stable and correct). A plausible-looking wrong value is worse than an error.

THE ARM MEASURES ITS OWN WALLS BETTER THAN A HUMAN DOES (2026-07-12, follower, PR #41). Once elbow_flex`s axis was linear (post re-zero), gentle_move was driven PAST the known travel and left to find each wall by feel — creep under torque, watch present_load, stop on contact, back off, hold. The blind-person-in-a-room primitive; the same one `arm explore` uses.

  arm flex elbow_flex --to  900 --gentle  -> contacted  988, load 500 (SATURATED)
  arm flex elbow_flex --to 3400 --gentle  -> contacted 3274, load 500 (SATURATED)

THE MACHINE BEAT THE HAND ON BOTH SIDES. Hand sweeps gave raw walls 218 / 2107 (and varied 206..218 depending on push force — a human stops when it FEELS firm). The arm gave 251 / 2061 — further out AND repeatable, because it presses to a fixed load threshold every time. Load SATURATING at the 500 torque cap is the signature of a real wall, not of an operator`s judgement. LESSON: for anything the arm can measure about its own body, let the arm measure it.

THE SECOND MOVE IS THE PROOF OF THE WHOLE RE-ZERO: commanding corrected 3400 CROSSES the raw 4095->0 boundary, and in the corrected frame it is a plain linear climb that converged. That exact command would have rotated the elbow THE LONG WAY ROUND into a wall before the re-zero.

DERIVED CONSTANT DISCIPLINE (the durable lesson): the arc table exists to be RE-MEASURED, but ~125 arc-coupled literals were hard-coded across two test files, so re-measuring it broke 34 tests. Tests now DERIVE every expectation from arm_spec (REZERO_ARCS, .midpoint, .evicts, _ARC_MARGIN_TICKS). ACID TEST: change the margin or either wall -> suite stays green. Same rule applied to prose: docstrings and the explain catalog no longer quote ticks AT ALL — a doc that names a measurement is a doc that goes stale, and `explain arm rezero` prints to whoever is standing at the arm.

ARC MARGIN IS LOAD-BEARING: a first cut declared the arc AT the extremes of one hand sweep and FALSE-REFUSED within minutes (the joint came to rest 11 ticks past an edge the operator had simply stopped short of). Declare the arc as a STRICT SUBSET of the unreachable region, inset on both sides. Conservative in both directions: cannot false-refuse a legal position, and cannot claim a tick the joint can actually reach. NOTE the operator had to move the gripper aside `or we would hit the table` — so a wall may be ENVIRONMENTAL, not mechanical, which makes true travel WIDER and the true arc NARROWER. Margin absorbs that too. This is issue #34`s `the table is the wall` arriving in a new place.

OPERATOR INSIGHT WORTH KEEPING: `there is the max of each joint, but also the max of a certain setup/joined movement`. That IS issue #34 — single-joint range vs COMBINATION reachability — and it remains unanswered until the grid lands.

MAJOR FINDING (2026-07-12, follower, code-driven limit probe of all six joints). ISSUE #35 IS NOT A TWO-JOINT PROBLEM — IT IS POTENTIALLY A SIX-JOINT PROBLEM, and the software bound has been HIDING it.

THE STRUCTURAL REASON: every joint except elbow_flex still carries the FACTORY offset Ofs=85. The commandable bound is reported tick 4095. With Ofs=85 that is RAW 84 — exactly ONE TICK BELOW THE SEAM, which lives at raw == Ofs == 85. So for every factory-offset joint, THE SOFTWARE BOUND LANDS PRECISELY ON THE SEAM. The arm has been reporting the seam as its boundary the whole time.

EVIDENCE — gentle_move probed each joint to both ends of its permitted range in one pose. Three joints reached the bound with NO CONTACT (still free), stopping this close to the seam in raw ticks:
  shoulder_lift : reported 4094 -> raw 83  (2 ticks from the seam)  NO WALL
  gripper       : reported 4093 -> raw 82  (3 ticks from the seam)  NO WALL
  shoulder_pan  : reported 4085 -> raw 74  (11 ticks from the seam) NO WALL
No contact means they could still have kept going — their remaining travel lies ACROSS the seam. elbow_flex is the ONLY honest row (real WALLS at both ends, 983 and 3267, with 829 ticks of clearance from its seam) precisely because its seam was moved out of its travel by the re-zero.

THIS EXPLAINS WHY THE REACHABILITY MAP WAS GARBAGE — not just the grid (#34), but the measurements feeding it.

CHICKEN-AND-EGG: to re-zero a joint you need its unreachable arc, but you cannot measure the arc until you can see PAST the seam. WAY OUT (already proven on elbow_flex): temporarily shift the offset to move the seam elsewhere, probe freely to find the TRUE travel, then compute the proper offset from that.

OPERATOR`S FRAMING, adopted: MECHANICAL limit = the WIDEST envelope ever recorded across configurations (an obstacle can only make a range smaller, never larger, so the max over poses converges on the mechanical truth). ENVIRONMENTAL limit = whatever is narrower in a given pose. Mechanical belongs in arm_spec (intrinsic, portable); environmental belongs in the map (pose-dependent, and NOT in the servo`s EEPROM).

ALSO PROVEN: elbow_flex`s walls are MECHANICAL, not the table. Probed in 3 poses (shoulder_lift 3354 vs 3779, wrist_flex 3963 vs 1366): wall at 988/986/984 low and 3274/3278 high — moves by <=4 ticks while other joints move by hundreds. Intrinsic.

GOTCHA on ticks/second from `arm profile`: a SHORT probe underestimates travel rate badly, because the ~155ms motion-onset latency dominates the timing. First run (221-tick probes) reported 105-228 ticks/s and the rate FELL as speed rose — a measurement artifact, not physics. Rate must be measured over a LONG travel.

RISK r2 IS REFUTED: contact detection still fired at EVERY speed from 150 to 1600 on elbow_flex (contact point repeatable to +/-2 ticks across 16 probes). Speed 150 is NOT the ceiling; no ceiling was found at all.

SPEC CONVERGED + PR #44 (2026-07-12, v0.22.2, /think -> /spec-to-plan): `arm limits` — the arm measures its own joint travel. Handles issue #43 (the software bound lands EXACTLY on the encoder seam for every factory-offset joint: reported 4095 under Ofs=85 is raw 84, one tick below the seam at raw 85).

THE MECHANISM — THE ROLLING FRAME. Breaks the chicken-and-egg (you cannot measure a joint's unreachable arc until you can see past the seam; you cannot evict the seam until you know the arc). Escape: write a TEMPORARY offset mapping the joint's CURRENT raw position to reported 2048 — the seam is then half a turn away, the farthest it can possibly be, with ~2048 clear ticks each side. Creep outward, and RE-CENTRE whenever the creep nears the reported bound. The seam is rolled ahead of the joint and never obstructs it, so travel is unbounded by the frame. Displacement accumulates in RAW ticks (frame-independent), so the measurement survives the frame moving underneath it. Termination by accumulated raw displacement: a full 4096 ticks with no wall => the joint is CONTINUOUS and no offset can ever help it — which is how the verb DERIVES wrist_roll's known verdict instead of being told it.

USER RULING 1 — NAME THE FAKE WALL HONESTLY. gentle_move calls contact when load is high AND the joint has stopped advancing. A gravity-loaded joint (shoulder_lift carries the whole arm) lifting against the 500 Torque_Limit cap can stall at high load with NOTHING in front of it. FOUR verdicts, carried PER END not per joint: WALL / TORQUE-LIMITED / EDGE / TIMEOUT. A TORQUE-LIMITED end is a LOWER BOUND, never a wall. KEY INSIGHT that makes naming it sufficient: this failure is SAFE — a torque-limited stall makes a range too NARROW, never too wide, so the arm UNDER-claims its reach. arm_spec therefore stores 'the widest envelope OBSERVED', not 'the mechanical limit'.

COROLLARY — THE MAX-OVER-POSES RULE IS ONLY HALF TRUE. 'Mechanical = the widest envelope across poses, because an obstacle can only ever make a range smaller' is SOUND for a WALL and FALSE for a TORQUE-LIMITED end: the arm's own weakness is not an obstacle you can pose your way out of — it shrinks the range in EVERY pose, so no number of poses ever escapes it. A torque-limited end stays a lower bound FOREVER and must never be promoted on pose evidence alone.

USER RULING 2 — PERSIST RAW TICKS EVERYWHERE. A re-zero invalidates every recorded tick ONLY BECAUSE we persist REPORTED ticks, which are a view through the current offset. RAW ticks are INVARIANT under a re-zero (the offset changes, the encoder does not). So arm_spec + the map store RAW; reported is a display view converted at the bus edge. This ELIMINATES the failure mode rather than detecting it — the calibration-identity machinery proposed in the #36 spec is NOT needed to protect data that cannot go stale. REZERO_ARCS already made this move in PR #41.

STOP-GATE (plan task t12): if the verb cannot re-derive the two answers we ALREADY know — elbow_flex => BOUNDED, wrist_roll => CONTINUOUS — from measurement alone, it is not measuring, it is guessing, and NO verdict it gives on the other four joints may be believed. Everything downstream halts and returns to the user.

FALSIFIABLE ACCUSATIONS the frame forced (each can prove this work unnecessary): (a) arm_spec._REZERO_UNNECESSARY asserts 'four of the six joints do not wrap' — if all four come back with the seam OUTSIDE their travel, the message was RIGHT and the claim is retracted; (b) >=1 joint's measured span must differ from its EEPROM span by >100 ticks — if none does, the rationale for blocking #34 on this work is FALSE and must be withdrawn.

PLAN: 15 tasks / 10 waves. Wave 0 = 4 file-disjoint pure tasks (raw-tick boundary, four-verdict record, crash-safe calibration journal, guard tests incl. 'nothing writes servo addr 9/11' — LeRobot's write_calibration DOES write them and must NOT be ported: addrs 9/11 are Min/Max_Position_Limit and CLAMP goals in servo mode, which would narrow the very reachable set this work recovers). Then the probe engine (serial: rolling frame -> creep -> classifier -> measured arcs), the verb, then 4 human-gated hardware waves.
